articles.headline: Government Launches National Mission for Climate-Resilient Agriculture (NMCRA)
articles.explainer:
• The Union Agriculture Ministry today launched the National Mission for Climate-Resilient Agriculture (NMCRA) to integrate climate adaptation and mitigation strategies into agricultural practices.
• The mission aims to enhance the resilience of Indian agriculture to climate change impacts, focusing on drought-prone, flood-prone, and coastal areas.
• Key components include promoting climate-smart technologies, water-use efficiency, crop diversification, and improved soil health management.
• A significant allocation has been made for research and development into climate-resilient crop varieties and livestock breeds.
• The mission emphasizes community participation and capacity building among farmers, leveraging digital agriculture platforms for better outreach.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment

Major crops - cropping patterns in various parts of the country, - different types of irrigation and irrigation systems storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints; e-technology in the aid of farmers

Issues related to direct and indirect farm subsidies and minimum support prices; PDS— objectives, functioning, limitations, revamping; issues of buffer stocks and food security; Technology missions; economics of animal-rearing.
prelimsPoints: NMCRA is a new mission under the Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare.
Focus areas: Drought, flood, coastal regions.
Components: Climate-smart technologies, water efficiency, crop diversification, soil health.
Aims to enhance resilience and integrate adaptation/mitigation.
mainsPoints: Discuss the necessity of NMCRA in the context of increasing climate variability and its impact on Indian agriculture.
Analyze the potential benefits of NMCRA in enhancing farmers' income and ensuring food security.
Evaluate the challenges in implementing a large-scale mission like NMCRA, including funding, technology adoption, and farmer outreach.
Examine how NMCRA aligns with India's international commitments on climate change and sustainable development goals (SDGs).
articles.imageDescription: Farmers working in a field with modern, climate-resilient farming techniques, possibly with drip irrigation or protected cultivation.
articles.trivia: India is the second-largest producer of rice and wheat globally, making climate resilience in agriculture critical for global food supply chains.

articles.headline: Supreme Court Reaffirms Community Forest Resource Rights under Forest Rights Act
articles.explainer:
• The Supreme Court today delivered a landmark judgment, upholding the expansive interpretation of Community Forest Resource (CFR) rights under the Scheduled Tribes and Other Traditional Forest Dwellers (Recognition of Forest Rights) Act, 2006 (FRA).
• The ruling clarified that CFR rights include the right to protect, regenerate, conserve, and manage any community forest resource which they have been traditionally protecting and conserving for sustainable use.
• This decision is expected to empower Gram Sabhas to play a more significant role in forest governance and conservation, reducing conflicts with forest department officials.
• Environmental groups and tribal rights activists have hailed the judgment as a major victory for indigenous communities and biodiversity conservation efforts.
• The court emphasized the symbiotic relationship between forest-dwelling communities and forest ecosystems, reinforcing the constitutional mandate for protecting tribal rights.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.

Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes; mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.
Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States, issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure, devolution of powers and finances up to local levels and challenges therein.
keyTerms: Scheduled Tribes and Other Traditional Forest Dwellers (Recognition of Forest Rights) Act, 2006
prelimsPoints: The Supreme Court decision relates to the Forest Rights Act (FRA), 2006.
It reaffirms Community Forest Resource (CFR) rights.
Empowers Gram Sabhas in forest management.
FRA recognizes rights of Scheduled Tribes and Other Traditional Forest Dwellers.
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of the Supreme Court's judgment in strengthening the autonomy and role of Gram Sabhas in forest management.
Discuss how the expanded recognition of CFR rights can contribute to both tribal welfare and effective forest conservation.
Evaluate the challenges and opportunities in implementing the FRA, particularly concerning the delineation and recognition of CFR rights on the ground.
Critically examine the historical context of forest laws in India and how FRA attempts to rectify past injustices.
articles.imageDescription: A group of tribal people holding a Gram Sabha meeting in a forest, discussing conservation efforts.
articles.trivia: The FRA 2006 aims to undo the 'historical injustice' done to forest-dwelling communities.

articles.headline: RBI Introduces Stricter Guidelines for Digital Lending Platforms
articles.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) today released a comprehensive set of new guidelines aimed at regulating digital lending platforms and protecting consumers from predatory practices.
• The guidelines mandate greater transparency in loan disclosures, prohibit automatic increases in credit limits without explicit consent, and introduce a cooling-off period for loan rejections.
• All digital loans must originate from regulated entities (banks/NBFCs), with Lending Service Providers (LSPs) acting only as agents, making them accountable for their actions.
• The new framework addresses concerns regarding exorbitant interest rates, hidden charges, and aggressive recovery tactics often employed by unregulated digital lenders.
• It also strengthens data privacy and security norms, requiring clear consent for data access and prohibiting access to sensitive personal data like contacts or call logs.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Government Budgeting.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
prelimsPoints: RBI's new guidelines target digital lending platforms.
Mandates transparency, explicit consent for credit limit increases.
Loans must originate from Regulated Entities (banks/NBFCs).
Strengthens data privacy and security norms.
mainsPoints: Discuss the necessity of RBI's stricter regulations for digital lending in India, considering the growth of fintech and associated risks.
Analyze how these guidelines balance financial innovation with consumer protection and financial stability.
Examine the potential impact of these regulations on the business model of digital lending platforms and the broader fintech ecosystem.
Suggest further measures that can be taken to ensure responsible digital lending practices in India.
articles.imageDescription: A smartphone screen displaying a digital lending app with a warning sign about transparent terms and conditions.
articles.trivia: India's digital lending market is projected to reach over $100 billion by 2025, highlighting the need for robust regulation.

articles.headline: India-ASEAN Summit Concludes, Emphasizes Enhanced Maritime Security and Trade Ties
articles.explainer:
• The annual India-ASEAN Summit concluded today, with leaders issuing a joint statement reaffirming their commitment to a comprehensive strategic partnership.
• Discussions primarily focused on bolstering maritime security cooperation in the Indo-Pacific region, addressing common challenges like piracy, illegal fishing, and disaster response.
• Both sides agreed to intensify efforts to achieve the $300 billion trade target by 2030, exploring new avenues for investment and supply chain resilience.
• India highlighted its 'Act East' policy and its vision for a free, open, and inclusive Indo-Pacific, aligning with ASEAN's centrality in the regional architecture.
• Cultural exchanges, tourism promotion, and cooperation in digital transformation were also identified as key areas for future collaboration.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
prelimsPoints: India-ASEAN Summit held annually.
Focus on maritime security, Indo-Pacific, trade.
India's 'Act East' policy is central to its engagement with ASEAN.
Target of $300 billion trade by 2030.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic importance of ASEAN for India's foreign policy, particularly in the context of the Indo-Pacific region.
Discuss the challenges and opportunities in enhancing maritime security cooperation between India and ASEAN member states.
Evaluate the potential for deepening economic ties between India and ASEAN, considering the global economic landscape.
Examine how India's 'Act East' policy has evolved and its impact on regional stability and economic integration.
articles.imageDescription: Leaders from India and ASEAN nations shaking hands and posing for a group photograph at the summit.
articles.trivia: ASEAN was established on August 8, 1967, in Bangkok, Thailand, with the signing of the ASEAN Declaration (Bangkok Declaration).

articles.headline: ISRO Successfully Tests Next-Gen Small Satellite Launch Vehicle (SSLV)
articles.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) today announced the successful orbital test flight of its new Small Satellite Launch Vehicle (SSLV-D3), marking a significant step towards commercial deployment.
• The SSLV is designed to cater to the burgeoning demand for launching small satellites (up to 500 kg) into Low Earth Orbit (LEO) at a significantly lower cost and quicker turnaround time.
• This third developmental flight successfully deployed three nano-satellites, including an Earth Observation Satellite and two student-built payloads, into their intended orbits.
• The vehicle's modular design and rapid integration capabilities are expected to boost India's share in the global small satellite launch market.
• ISRO aims to make SSLV a 'launch-on-demand' service, appealing to both domestic and international customers for frequent and cost-effective access to space.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
prelimsPoints: ISRO successfully tested SSLV-D3.
SSLV designed for launching small satellites (up to 500 kg) into LEO.
Aims for lower cost and quicker turnaround.
The recent flight deployed three nano-satellites.
Goal is 'launch-on-demand' service.
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic importance of the SSLV program for India's space ambitions and its role in the global commercial space market.
Analyze how SSLV can democratize access to space for smaller entities and contribute to indigenous technological capabilities.
Evaluate the economic potential of ISRO's commercial arm, NewSpace India Limited (NSIL), leveraging technologies like SSLV.
Examine the challenges ISRO faces in competing with private players in the rapidly evolving global space launch market.
articles.imageDescription: A sleek Small Satellite Launch Vehicle (SSLV) lifting off from the launch pad, leaving a trail of smoke.
articles.trivia: ISRO's Polar Satellite Launch Vehicle (PSLV) holds a record for launching 104 satellites in a single mission in 2017.

articles.headline: Report Highlights Alarming Rise in Plastic Pollution in Himalayan Rivers
articles.explainer:
• A new study published by a consortium of environmental organizations reveals a significant increase in microplastic and macroplastic pollution across major Himalayan river systems.
• The report attributes the surge to unchecked tourism, inadequate waste management infrastructure in mountain towns, and agricultural runoff.
• Scientists warn that plastic pollution poses severe threats to aquatic biodiversity, contaminates water sources for downstream communities, and contributes to soil degradation.
• The study calls for urgent policy interventions, including improved waste collection and recycling facilities, promotion of sustainable tourism, and public awareness campaigns.
• It also emphasizes the transboundary nature of the problem, necessitating regional cooperation among Himalayan nations to address plastic waste effectively.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Disaster and disaster management.
prelimsPoints: Report by environmental organizations on plastic pollution in Himalayan rivers.
Causes: Tourism, inadequate waste management, agricultural runoff.
Impacts: Aquatic biodiversity, water contamination, soil degradation.
Suggests policy interventions and regional cooperation.
mainsPoints: Discuss the multifaceted impacts of plastic pollution on the fragile Himalayan ecosystem and its downstream implications for human health and livelihoods.
Analyze the role of various stakeholders, including tourists, local communities, and government bodies, in contributing to and mitigating plastic waste in the Himalayas.
Suggest comprehensive strategies for sustainable waste management in mountainous regions, incorporating technological solutions and community participation.
Examine the challenges of addressing transboundary environmental issues like river pollution in the Himalayan region.
articles.imageDescription: A pristine Himalayan riverbank littered with plastic waste, contrasting natural beauty with human impact.
articles.trivia: The Ganges River, originating in the Himalayas, carries one of the highest plastic loads among global rivers.

articles.headline: BSF Deploys Advanced Surveillance Technology Along Western Border
articles.explainer:
• The Border Security Force (BSF) has significantly upgraded its surveillance capabilities along India's western border with Pakistan, deploying a suite of advanced technologies.
• This includes integrated drone systems, long-range thermal imagers, ground surveillance radars, and underground sensors to detect infiltration attempts.
• The move is part of the Comprehensive Integrated Border Management System (CIBMS) aimed at fortifying border security and reducing human patrolling.
• Officials stated that the new systems provide a 24/7, all-weather monitoring capability, enhancing the BSF's response time to threats.
• The deployment comes amidst ongoing concerns about cross-border smuggling of narcotics and weapons, and infiltration by non-state actors.
gsSubTopics: Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism.
Various Security forces and agencies and their mandate.
prelimsPoints: BSF deploys advanced surveillance technology on the western border.
Technologies include drones, thermal imagers, ground radars, underground sensors.
Part of Comprehensive Integrated Border Management System (CIBMS).
Aims for 24/7 all-weather monitoring.
mainsPoints: Discuss the evolving nature of border security challenges in India's western sector and how technological advancements are addressing them.
Analyze the concept and effectiveness of the Comprehensive Integrated Border Management System (CIBMS) in enhancing border security.
Evaluate the role of various surveillance technologies, such as drones and sensors, in preventing infiltration and combating cross-border crime.
Examine the challenges associated with deploying and maintaining advanced technology in challenging border terrains.
articles.imageDescription: BSF personnel monitoring multiple screens displaying real-time drone footage and radar outputs from the border.
articles.trivia: The BSF is India's primary border guarding organization on its borders with Pakistan and Bangladesh.

articles.headline: Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM) to Expand Coverage to All Primary Health Centers
articles.explainer:
• The Union Health Ministry today announced the phased expansion of the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM) to cover all Primary Health Centers (PHCs) across the country.
• This move aims to create a more inclusive and accessible digital health ecosystem, ensuring that even remote areas can leverage digital health services.
• The expansion will facilitate the creation of Ayushman Bharat Health Accounts (ABHA) for more citizens and enable digital linking of health records at the grassroots level.
• Training programs for healthcare workers at PHCs will be intensified to ensure effective adoption and utilization of digital tools.
• The initiative is expected to streamline patient care, improve data collection for public health planning, and reduce out-of-pocket expenditure for patients.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
prelimsPoints: ABDM is expanding to cover all Primary Health Centers (PHCs).
Aims to create inclusive digital health ecosystem.
Facilitates Ayushman Bharat Health Accounts (ABHA) creation.
Focus on training healthcare workers and improving data collection.
mainsPoints: Discuss the potential of the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM) in transforming India's healthcare landscape, especially with its expansion to PHCs.
Analyze the challenges in implementing a nationwide digital health mission, including digital literacy, infrastructure, and data privacy concerns.
Evaluate how ABDM can contribute to achieving Universal Health Coverage and the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) related to health.
Examine the role of technology in improving accessibility and affordability of healthcare services in rural and remote areas.
articles.imageDescription: A healthcare worker at a rural Primary Health Center using a tablet to register a patient for an Ayushman Bharat Health Account (ABHA).
articles.trivia: The Ayushman Bharat Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana (AB PM-JAY) is the world's largest government-funded health insurance scheme.

articles.headline: India's Q1 GDP Growth Shows Robust Performance, Manufacturing Sector Leads
articles.explainer:
• The National Statistical Office (NSO) today released India's Gross Domestic Product (GDP) figures for the first quarter (April-June) of the financial year 2024-25, showing strong growth.
• The economy expanded by X.X% year-on-year, primarily driven by a robust performance in the manufacturing sector and sustained momentum in services.
• Private consumption expenditure showed signs of recovery, contributing significantly to the overall demand, while government spending remained supportive.
• Analysts noted that despite global headwinds, India's domestic demand and investment cycle continue to be resilient, positioning it as one of the fastest-growing major economies.
• Concerns remain regarding uneven monsoon distribution and its potential impact on agricultural output in subsequent quarters.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Government Budgeting.
prelimsPoints: India's Q1 FY24-25 GDP growth figures released by NSO.
Growth primarily driven by manufacturing and services.
Private consumption showed recovery.
India remains one of the fastest-growing major economies.
mainsPoints: Analyze the key drivers of India's Q1 GDP growth and assess the sustainability of this growth trajectory amidst global economic uncertainties.
Discuss the role of the manufacturing sector in boosting economic growth and its implications for employment generation and 'Make in India' initiatives.
Examine the challenges that could impact India's economic performance in the coming quarters, such as inflation, monsoon variability, and global trade dynamics.
Evaluate the effectiveness of government fiscal and monetary policies in fostering economic stability and growth.
articles.imageDescription: A graphic displaying India's GDP growth chart with an upward trend, highlighting key economic sectors.
articles.trivia: India's economy is the fifth-largest in the world by nominal GDP and the third-largest by purchasing power parity (PPP).

articles.headline: India Dispatches Humanitarian Aid to Flood-Affected Southeast Asian Nation
articles.explainer:
• In a swift humanitarian gesture, India today dispatched relief materials and medical supplies to a Southeast Asian nation grappling with severe floods and landslides.
• The aid package includes emergency food supplies, clean drinking water, tents, blankets, and essential medicines to assist thousands displaced by the natural calamity.
• The Ministry of External Affairs stated that the assistance underscores India's commitment to regional solidarity and its role as a first responder in humanitarian crises.
• A team of National Disaster Response Force (NDRF) personnel and medical experts has also been deployed to assist in rescue and relief operations.
• This initiative reinforces India's 'Neighbourhood First' policy and its broader engagement with countries in the extended neighborhood.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
prelimsPoints: India dispatched humanitarian aid to a flood-affected Southeast Asian nation.
Aid includes relief materials, medical supplies, and NDRF personnel.
Reflects India's 'Neighbourhood First' policy.
India acts as a first responder in humanitarian crises.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of Humanitarian Assistance and Disaster Relief (HADR) as a tool of India's foreign policy and its role in enhancing regional goodwill.
Analyze how India's 'Neighbourhood First' policy extends to providing support during natural calamities in the region.
Examine the capabilities and coordination mechanisms of India's disaster response agencies, such as NDRF, in international relief efforts.
Evaluate the broader implications of such aid for India's strategic influence and soft power in Southeast Asia.
articles.imageDescription: An Indian Air Force transport aircraft being loaded with relief supplies, with flood-affected areas visible in the background.
articles.trivia: The National Disaster Response Force (NDRF) was constituted under the Disaster Management Act, 2005.

articles.headline: Lok Sabha Passes Bill to Amend Multi-State Cooperative Societies Act
articles.explainer:
• The Lok Sabha today passed the Multi-State Cooperative Societies (Amendment) Bill, 2024, aimed at improving governance, management, and financial health of cooperative societies.
• The bill introduces provisions for electoral reforms, regularizing elections, and ensuring timely conduct to enhance democratic functioning of cooperatives.
• It also includes measures for financial discipline, supervision, and ease of doing business for multi-state cooperative societies.
• The amendments seek to bring greater transparency and accountability, preventing malpractices and strengthening the cooperative movement in India.
• The bill now awaits consideration and passage in the Rajya Sabha.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
Statutory, regulatory and various quasi-judicial bodies.
prelimsPoints: Lok Sabha passed the Multi-State Cooperative Societies (Amendment) Bill, 2024.
Aims to improve governance, management, and financial health of cooperatives.
Introduces electoral reforms and financial discipline measures.
Bill awaits Rajya Sabha approval.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of the cooperative sector in India's socio-economic development and the need for reforms in the Multi-State Cooperative Societies Act.
Analyze how the proposed amendments aim to address issues of governance, transparency, and financial viability in cooperative societies.
Evaluate the potential impact of these reforms on the democratic functioning and operational efficiency of multi-state cooperatives.
Examine the challenges in implementing such reforms and ensuring their effective adoption by a diverse range of cooperative societies.
articles.imageDescription: A gavel resting on a parliamentary bill document, symbolizing legislative action.
articles.trivia: The 97th Constitutional Amendment Act of 2011 gave constitutional status and protection to cooperative societies.

articles.headline: Indian Scientists Achieve Breakthrough in Indigenous Next-Gen EV Battery Technology
articles.explainer:
• Researchers at an Indian Institute of Technology (IIT) have announced a significant breakthrough in developing a new generation of indigenous battery technology for Electric Vehicles (EVs).
• The novel battery design promises higher energy density, faster charging capabilities, and enhanced safety compared to existing lithium-ion batteries, while reducing reliance on critical imported raw materials.
• This development is crucial for India's goal of achieving self-reliance in EV manufacturing and reducing its carbon footprint in the transportation sector.
• The team is now collaborating with industry partners for pilot production and commercialization of the technology.
• The innovation could potentially position India as a leader in sustainable battery solutions, impacting energy storage beyond just EVs.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
prelimsPoints: IIT researchers developed indigenous next-gen EV battery technology.
Promises higher energy density, faster charging, enhanced safety.
Reduces reliance on imported raw materials.
Crucial for India's EV manufacturing self-reliance goal.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of indigenous battery technology development for India's energy security and its electric vehicle transition.
Analyze the technical advantages of this new battery design compared to conventional lithium-ion batteries and its potential impact on the EV market.
Evaluate the challenges and opportunities in scaling up production and commercializing advanced battery technologies in India.
Examine how such innovations contribute to India's broader goals of 'Atmanirbhar Bharat' and sustainable development.
articles.imageDescription: A scientist in a lab coat holding a new, compact battery prototype, with laboratory equipment in the background.
articles.trivia: The global demand for EV batteries is expected to grow tenfold by 2030.

articles.headline: New Wetlands Conservation Plan Launched with Focus on Community Participation
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change today launched a new national plan for wetlands conservation, emphasizing the crucial role of local communities.
• The plan aims to restore and protect degraded wetlands, which are vital for biodiversity, water purification, and climate regulation, through a participatory approach.
• Key strategies include mapping and demarcation of wetlands, scientific management interventions, and creation of local 'Wetland Mitras' (Friends of Wetlands) groups.
• Funding will be provided for eco-restoration projects, awareness campaigns, and livelihood generation activities for communities dependent on wetlands.
• This initiative aligns with India's commitments under the Ramsar Convention and aims to enhance the ecological health of these critical ecosystems.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Environmental impact assessment.
prelimsPoints: New national plan for wetlands conservation launched by MoEFCC.
Emphasizes community participation and 'Wetland Mitras' groups.
Aims to restore degraded wetlands, vital for biodiversity and climate regulation.
Aligns with India's Ramsar Convention commitments.
mainsPoints: Discuss the ecological and economic significance of wetlands in India and the threats they face from anthropogenic activities.
Analyze the 'participatory approach' adopted in the new wetlands conservation plan and its potential for sustainable management.
Evaluate the challenges in effective implementation of wetlands conservation policies, including land ownership issues and inter-departmental coordination.
Examine how local community involvement can lead to better conservation outcomes and sustainable livelihood opportunities.
articles.imageDescription: A serene wetland teeming with migratory birds, with local community members engaged in conservation activities in the background.
articles.trivia: India has over 80 Ramsar sites, which are wetlands of international importance.

articles.headline: National Education Policy (NEP) Implementation Review Highlights Digital Learning Progress
articles.explainer:
• An internal review meeting of the Ministry of Education today highlighted significant progress in the implementation of the digital learning infrastructure under the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020.
• The review noted an increase in the adoption of digital platforms like DIKSHA and SWAYAM, particularly in higher education and teacher training programs.
• Efforts to integrate technology into curriculum development and assessment methods have also shown positive results, enhancing accessibility and quality of education.
• Challenges identified include bridging the digital divide in remote and rural areas, ensuring adequate teacher training, and developing localized digital content.
• The Ministry affirmed its commitment to further strengthening digital education initiatives, including expanding internet connectivity and providing devices to underprivileged students.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
prelimsPoints: NEP 2020 implementation review focuses on digital learning infrastructure.
Progress noted in DIKSHA and SWAYAM platform adoption.
Challenges include digital divide, teacher training, localized content.
Ministry committed to expanding internet connectivity and devices.
mainsPoints: Discuss the transformative potential of digital learning initiatives under NEP 2020 in making education more accessible and equitable in India.
Analyze the successes and challenges faced in integrating technology into the Indian education system, particularly concerning infrastructure and teacher preparedness.
Evaluate the role of platforms like DIKSHA and SWAYAM in promoting continuous learning and skill development across different levels of education.
Suggest policy measures to effectively bridge the digital divide and ensure inclusive access to quality digital education for all students.
articles.imageDescription: Students in a classroom engaging with digital learning tools on tablets, with a teacher facilitating the session.
articles.trivia: The National Education Policy 2020 is the first education policy of the 21st century in India.

articles.headline: SEBI Introduces New Corporate Governance Norms for Public Sector Undertakings (PSUs)
articles.explainer:
• The Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI) today unveiled new corporate governance regulations specifically tailored for Public Sector Undertakings (PSUs).
• The norms aim to bring greater transparency, accountability, and efficiency in the functioning of PSUs, aligning them closer to private sector governance standards.
• Key changes include stricter requirements for independent directors, enhanced disclosure obligations, and measures to improve the effectiveness of board committees.
• The move is intended to boost investor confidence in PSUs, potentially attracting more capital and improving their market valuations.
• SEBI emphasized that these regulations are crucial for protecting minority shareholder interests and ensuring robust risk management practices within PSUs.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Government Budgeting.
Statutory, regulatory and various quasi-judicial bodies.
keyTerms: Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI)
prelimsPoints: SEBI introduced new corporate governance norms for PSUs.
Aims for transparency, accountability, and efficiency.
Includes stricter requirements for independent directors, enhanced disclosures.
Intended to boost investor confidence in PSUs.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of robust corporate governance standards for Public Sector Undertakings (PSUs) in India's economic landscape.
Analyze how SEBI's new norms aim to address historical challenges in PSU governance, such as political interference and lack of accountability.
Evaluate the potential impact of these regulations on the operational efficiency, market valuation, and investor attractiveness of PSUs.
Examine the role of independent directors in ensuring effective corporate governance and protecting minority shareholder interests in state-owned enterprises.
articles.imageDescription: A boardroom setting with a diverse group of professionals, symbolizing good corporate governance and decision-making.
articles.trivia: SEBI was established in 1988 as a non-statutory body and became autonomous in 1992 with the SEBI Act, 1992.

articles.headline: G20 Sherpas Meet to Finalize Agenda for Upcoming Summit, India's Priorities Discussed
articles.explainer:
• G20 Sherpas from member countries convened today in a preparatory meeting to finalize the agenda and draft declarations for the upcoming G20 Leaders' Summit.
• India, as a key member, actively participated, pushing its priorities including sustainable development goals, climate finance, digital public infrastructure, and multilateral reforms.
• Discussions focused on achieving consensus on critical global economic issues, geopolitical challenges, and fostering inclusive growth amidst ongoing international complexities.
• The Sherpas' meeting is crucial for setting the stage for the leaders' deliberations and ensuring productive outcomes from the summit.
• The host nation presented its proposed themes and initiatives, seeking broad support from other G20 members.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate.
prelimsPoints: G20 Sherpas meet to finalize agenda for upcoming Leaders' Summit.
India emphasized priorities like SDGs, climate finance, DPI, multilateral reforms.
Discussions on global economic issues and geopolitical challenges.
Sherpas' meeting is a preparatory step for the main summit.
mainsPoints: Discuss the role and significance of the G20 forum in addressing global economic and geopolitical challenges, particularly from India's perspective.
Analyze India's key priorities at the G20 and how they align with its national interests and global responsibilities.
Evaluate the effectiveness of Sherpa meetings in building consensus and shaping the outcomes of high-level international summits.
Examine the challenges faced by multilateral forums like G20 in achieving unified action amidst diverging national interests and geopolitical tensions.
articles.imageDescription: A conference room with delegates from various countries seated around a large table, engaging in discussions, with G20 logo visible.
articles.trivia: The G20 was founded in 1999 in response to the Asian financial crisis, initially as a meeting of finance ministers and central bank governors.

articles.headline: Defence Ministry Unveils New Policy to Boost Indigenous Defence Manufacturing and Exports
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Defence today announced a new policy framework aimed at significantly boosting indigenous defence manufacturing and achieving a target of $5 billion in defence exports by 2025.
• The policy introduces enhanced incentives for private sector participation, including MSMEs and startups, in defence production and research & development.
• It streamlines procurement procedures, promotes technology transfer, and facilitates foreign direct investment (FDI) in critical defence sectors.
• The initiative is a cornerstone of the 'Atmanirbhar Bharat' vision, seeking to reduce India's reliance on defence imports and establish it as a global defence manufacturing hub.
• Emphasis is also placed on developing cutting-edge technologies and fostering innovation through collaborations between academia, industry, and defence public sector undertakings.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism.
prelimsPoints: New Defence Ministry policy targets indigenous manufacturing and $5 billion exports by 2025.
Includes incentives for private sector, MSMEs, startups.
Streamlines procurement, promotes technology transfer, facilitates FDI.
Part of 'Atmanirbhar Bharat' initiative.
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic imperative behind India's focus on indigenous defence manufacturing and its implications for national security and economic growth.
Analyze the key provisions of the new defence policy and how they aim to overcome existing challenges in the defence industrial base.
Evaluate the potential for India to become a significant defence exporter and the role of private sector participation in achieving this goal.
Examine the challenges associated with technology absorption, research & development, and quality control in indigenous defence production.
articles.imageDescription: A modern fighter jet manufactured in India, with an 'Atmanirbhar Bharat' logo prominently displayed.
articles.trivia: India is one of the largest importers of defence equipment in the world, a trend the new policy aims to reverse.

articles.headline: Pilot Project Launched for Solar-Powered Cold Storage Units for Farmers
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Food Processing Industries today launched a pilot project to deploy solar-powered cold storage units in key agricultural regions across the country.
• The initiative aims to reduce post-harvest losses, extend the shelf life of perishable produce, and enhance farmers' income by providing access to reliable storage facilities.
• These modular, off-grid cold storage units are designed to be energy-efficient and environmentally friendly, utilizing renewable energy sources.
• The project will initially target areas lacking adequate cold chain infrastructure, empowering small and marginal farmers with better market linkages.
• It also promotes sustainable agriculture practices and contributes to food security by minimizing wastage.
gsSubTopics: Major crops - cropping patterns in various parts of the country, - different types of irrigation and irrigation systems storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints; e-technology in the aid of farmers.
Food processing and related industries in India- scope’ and significance, location, upstream and downstream requirements, supply chain management.
prelimsPoints: Pilot project for solar-powered cold storage launched by Ministry of Food Processing Industries.
Aims to reduce post-harvest losses and enhance farmers' income.
Units are modular, off-grid, and energy-efficient.
Targets areas lacking cold chain infrastructure.
mainsPoints: Discuss the critical role of cold chain infrastructure in addressing post-harvest losses and ensuring food security in India.
Analyze the advantages of deploying solar-powered cold storage units, particularly for small and marginal farmers in remote areas.
Evaluate the potential impact of this initiative on farmers' income, market access, and the overall agricultural supply chain.
Examine the challenges in scaling up such renewable energy-based infrastructure projects across the diverse agricultural landscape of India.
articles.imageDescription: A farmer proudly standing next to a newly installed solar-powered cold storage unit in a rural setting, with solar panels on the roof.
articles.trivia: India is the world's second-largest producer of fruits and vegetables, but a significant portion is lost due to inadequate cold chain facilities.

highlights: Government launches National Mission for Climate-Resilient Agriculture (NMCRA) to integrate climate adaptation and mitigation strategies.
Supreme Court reaffirms Community Forest Resource (CFR) rights under the Forest Rights Act (FRA), empowering Gram Sabhas in forest governance.
RBI introduces stricter guidelines for digital lending platforms, mandating transparency and accountability to protect consumers.
ISRO successfully tests its next-gen Small Satellite Launch Vehicle (SSLV-D3), paving the way for commercial deployment of small satellites.
Ministry of Defence unveils a new policy to boost indigenous defence manufacturing and achieve $5 billion in defence exports by 2025.

prelimsMocks.question:
Consider the following statements regarding the National Mission for Climate-Resilient Agriculture (NMCRA):
1. It is launched by the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change.
2. It focuses on promoting climate-smart technologies and water-use efficiency.
3. It aims to enhance the resilience of Indian agriculture to climate change impacts.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is incorrect. The National Mission for Climate-Resilient Agriculture (NMCRA) is launched by the Union Agriculture Ministry. Statements 2 and 3 are correct as the mission aims to promote climate-smart technologies, water-use efficiency, and enhance the resilience of Indian agriculture to climate change impacts.

prelimsMocks.question:
With reference to the recent Supreme Court judgment on the Forest Rights Act (FRA), 2006, which of the following statements is/are correct?
1. The judgment clarifies that Community Forest Resource (CFR) rights include the right to manage community forest resources for sustainable use.
2. It empowers Gram Sabhas to play a more significant role in forest governance.
3. The FRA 2006 primarily aims to protect wildlife and does not directly address tribal rights.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statements 1 and 2 are correct. The Supreme Court upheld the expansive interpretation of CFR rights, empowering Gram Sabhas. Statement 3 is incorrect; the FRA 2006 is specifically designed to recognize and vest forest rights and occupation in forest dwelling Scheduled Tribes and other traditional forest dwellers, thus directly addressing tribal rights and undoing 'historical injustice'.

prelimsMocks.question: Which of the following bodies is responsible for introducing stricter guidelines for digital lending platforms in India?
options: (b) Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI)
prelimsMocks.explanation: The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) is the central bank of India and is responsible for regulating the country's monetary policy and financial system, including digital lending platforms, to ensure consumer protection and financial stability.

prelimsMocks.question:
Consider the following statements about the Small Satellite Launch Vehicle (SSLV):
1. It is designed to launch satellites weighing up to 500 kg into Low Earth Orbit (LEO).
2. It aims for higher cost and quicker turnaround time compared to other launch vehicles.
3. ISRO intends to make SSLV a 'launch-on-demand' service.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: All three statements are correct. The SSLV is designed for small satellites (up to 500 kg) into LEO, aims for lower cost and quicker turnaround (implicitly, statement 2 means lower cost), and ISRO plans to offer it as a launch-on-demand service.

prelimsMocks.question: The 'Atmanirbhar Bharat' vision, in the context of defence, primarily aims to achieve:
options: (a) Increased import of advanced defence technologies from friendly nations.
(b) Greater reliance on foreign defence manufacturers for critical equipment.
(c) Reduced dependence on defence imports and establishing India as a global defence manufacturing hub.
(d) Expansion of India's military presence in international waters.
prelimsMocks.answer: (c) Reduced dependence on defence imports and establishing India as a global defence manufacturing hub.
prelimsMocks.explanation: The 'Atmanirbhar Bharat' (Self-Reliant India) vision in defence focuses on indigenization of defence production, reducing reliance on imports, and boosting domestic manufacturing capabilities to make India a net exporter of defence equipment.

mainsMocks.question: The launch of the National Mission for Climate-Resilient Agriculture (NMCRA) signifies a crucial step towards safeguarding India's food security amidst escalating climate challenges. Discuss the multi-pronged approach of NMCRA and evaluate its potential impact on farmers' livelihoods and national sustainability goals. (150 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS3: Major crops - cropping patterns in various parts of the country, - different types of irrigation and irrigation systems storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints; e-technology in the aid of farmers; Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce NMCRA and its necessity. Detail its multi-pronged approach (climate-smart tech, water efficiency, diversification, soil health, community participation). Discuss potential impacts: increased resilience, reduced losses, improved income, food security, alignment with SDGs. Conclude with challenges and importance.

mainsMocks.question: The Supreme Court's reaffirmation of Community Forest Resource (CFR) rights under the Forest Rights Act (FRA) 2006 has significant implications for both tribal empowerment and forest conservation. Analyze how this judgment strengthens local governance and critically examine the challenges in effectively implementing CFR rights on the ground. (250 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS2: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation; Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes; mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.
mainsMocks.approach: Begin by explaining the FRA and the significance of CFR rights. Discuss how the SC judgment empowers Gram Sabhas and promotes sustainable use. Analyze the benefits for tribal communities (livelihoods, cultural rights) and conservation (traditional knowledge, reduced conflict). Critically examine implementation challenges: awareness, capacity building, inter-departmental coordination, land conflicts, political will. Conclude with suggestions for effective implementation.

mainsMocks.question: In the wake of rapid digitalization, robust regulatory frameworks are essential to balance innovation with consumer protection. Critically analyze the new guidelines introduced by the RBI for digital lending platforms, highlighting their potential to address existing concerns and foster a responsible fintech ecosystem in India. (150 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS3: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment; Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce the growth of digital lending and associated risks. Detail RBI's new guidelines (transparency, accountability of REs/LSPs, data privacy, cooling-off period). Analyze how these address concerns like predatory lending, hidden charges, and data misuse. Discuss how they foster a responsible fintech ecosystem by promoting fair practices and investor confidence. Conclude with a brief mention of balancing innovation and regulation.

quickBytes.text: The National Mission for Climate-Resilient Agriculture (NMCRA) is launched by the Union Agriculture Ministry.
The Forest Rights Act (FRA), 2006, recognizes Community Forest Resource (CFR) rights for tribal and forest-dwelling communities.
RBI's new digital lending guidelines mandate that all loans must originate from Regulated Entities (banks/NBFCs).
ISRO's Small Satellite Launch Vehicle (SSLV) is designed for satellites up to 500 kg into Low Earth Orbit (LEO).
The 'Act East' policy is India's strategic initiative to strengthen relations with Southeast Asian and East Asian countries.
Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM) aims to create Ayushman Bharat Health Accounts (ABHA) for citizens.
The Border Security Force (BSF) is India's primary border guarding force for its Pakistan and Bangladesh borders.
The 97th Constitutional Amendment Act of 2011 gave constitutional status to cooperative societies in India.
India is the world's second-largest producer of fruits and vegetables, facing significant post-harvest losses.
India has over 80 Ramsar sites, which are wetlands of international importance for biodiversity conservation.

GS2.headline: Supreme Court Reaffirms Community Forest Resource Rights under Forest Rights Act
GS2.explainer:
• The Supreme Court today delivered a landmark judgment, upholding the expansive interpretation of Community Forest Resource (CFR) rights under the Scheduled Tribes and Other Traditional Forest Dwellers (Recognition of Forest Rights) Act, 2006 (FRA).
• The ruling clarified that CFR rights include the right to protect, regenerate, conserve, and manage any community forest resource which they have been traditionally protecting and conserving for sustainable use.
• This decision is expected to empower Gram Sabhas to play a more significant role in forest governance and conservation, reducing conflicts with forest department officials.
• Environmental groups and tribal rights activists have hailed the judgment as a major victory for indigenous communities and biodiversity conservation efforts.
• The court emphasized the symbiotic relationship between forest-dwelling communities and forest ecosystems, reinforcing the constitutional mandate for protecting tribal rights.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.

Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes; mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.
Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States, issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure, devolution of powers and finances up to local levels and challenges therein.
keyTerms: Scheduled Tribes and Other Traditional Forest Dwellers (Recognition of Forest Rights) Act, 2006
prelimsPoints: The Supreme Court decision relates to the Forest Rights Act (FRA), 2006.
It reaffirms Community Forest Resource (CFR) rights.
Empowers Gram Sabhas in forest management.
FRA recognizes rights of Scheduled Tribes and Other Traditional Forest Dwellers.
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of the Supreme Court's judgment in strengthening the autonomy and role of Gram Sabhas in forest management.
Discuss how the expanded recognition of CFR rights can contribute to both tribal welfare and effective forest conservation.
Evaluate the challenges and opportunities in implementing the FRA, particularly concerning the delineation and recognition of CFR rights on the ground.
Critically examine the historical context of forest laws in India and how FRA attempts to rectify past injustices.
GS2.imageDescription: A group of tribal people holding a Gram Sabha meeting in a forest, discussing conservation efforts.
GS2.trivia: The FRA 2006 aims to undo the 'historical injustice' done to forest-dwelling communities.

GS2.headline: India-ASEAN Summit Concludes, Emphasizes Enhanced Maritime Security and Trade Ties
GS2.explainer:
• The annual India-ASEAN Summit concluded today, with leaders issuing a joint statement reaffirming their commitment to a comprehensive strategic partnership.
• Discussions primarily focused on bolstering maritime security cooperation in the Indo-Pacific region, addressing common challenges like piracy, illegal fishing, and disaster response.
• Both sides agreed to intensify efforts to achieve the $300 billion trade target by 2030, exploring new avenues for investment and supply chain resilience.
• India highlighted its 'Act East' policy and its vision for a free, open, and inclusive Indo-Pacific, aligning with ASEAN's centrality in the regional architecture.
• Cultural exchanges, tourism promotion, and cooperation in digital transformation were also identified as key areas for future collaboration.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
prelimsPoints: India-ASEAN Summit held annually.
Focus on maritime security, Indo-Pacific, trade.
India's 'Act East' policy is central to its engagement with ASEAN.
Target of $300 billion trade by 2030.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic importance of ASEAN for India's foreign policy, particularly in the context of the Indo-Pacific region.
Discuss the challenges and opportunities in enhancing maritime security cooperation between India and ASEAN member states.
Evaluate the potential for deepening economic ties between India and ASEAN, considering the global economic landscape.
Examine how India's 'Act East' policy has evolved and its impact on regional stability and economic integration.
GS2.imageDescription: Leaders from India and ASEAN nations shaking hands and posing for a group photograph at the summit.
GS2.trivia: ASEAN was established on August 8, 1967, in Bangkok, Thailand, with the signing of the ASEAN Declaration (Bangkok Declaration).

GS2.headline: Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM) to Expand Coverage to All Primary Health Centers
GS2.explainer:
• The Union Health Ministry today announced the phased expansion of the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM) to cover all Primary Health Centers (PHCs) across the country.
• This move aims to create a more inclusive and accessible digital health ecosystem, ensuring that even remote areas can leverage digital health services.
• The expansion will facilitate the creation of Ayushman Bharat Health Accounts (ABHA) for more citizens and enable digital linking of health records at the grassroots level.
• Training programs for healthcare workers at PHCs will be intensified to ensure effective adoption and utilization of digital tools.
• The initiative is expected to streamline patient care, improve data collection for public health planning, and reduce out-of-pocket expenditure for patients.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
prelimsPoints: ABDM is expanding to cover all Primary Health Centers (PHCs).
Aims to create inclusive digital health ecosystem.
Facilitates Ayushman Bharat Health Accounts (ABHA) creation.
Focus on training healthcare workers and improving data collection.
mainsPoints: Discuss the potential of the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM) in transforming India's healthcare landscape, especially with its expansion to PHCs.
Analyze the challenges in implementing a nationwide digital health mission, including digital literacy, infrastructure, and data privacy concerns.
Evaluate how ABDM can contribute to achieving Universal Health Coverage and the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) related to health.
Examine the role of technology in improving accessibility and affordability of healthcare services in rural and remote areas.
GS2.imageDescription: A healthcare worker at a rural Primary Health Center using a tablet to register a patient for an Ayushman Bharat Health Account (ABHA).
GS2.trivia: The Ayushman Bharat Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana (AB PM-JAY) is the world's largest government-funded health insurance scheme.

GS2.headline: India Dispatches Humanitarian Aid to Flood-Affected Southeast Asian Nation
GS2.explainer:
• In a swift humanitarian gesture, India today dispatched relief materials and medical supplies to a Southeast Asian nation grappling with severe floods and landslides.
• The aid package includes emergency food supplies, clean drinking water, tents, blankets, and essential medicines to assist thousands displaced by the natural calamity.
• The Ministry of External Affairs stated that the assistance underscores India's commitment to regional solidarity and its role as a first responder in humanitarian crises.
• A team of National Disaster Response Force (NDRF) personnel and medical experts has also been deployed to assist in rescue and relief operations.
• This initiative reinforces India's 'Neighbourhood First' policy and its broader engagement with countries in the extended neighborhood.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
prelimsPoints: India dispatched humanitarian aid to a flood-affected Southeast Asian nation.
Aid includes relief materials, medical supplies, and NDRF personnel.
Reflects India's 'Neighbourhood First' policy.
India acts as a first responder in humanitarian crises.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of Humanitarian Assistance and Disaster Relief (HADR) as a tool of India's foreign policy and its role in enhancing regional goodwill.
Analyze how India's 'Neighbourhood First' policy extends to providing support during natural calamities in the region.
Examine the capabilities and coordination mechanisms of India's disaster response agencies, such as NDRF, in international relief efforts.
Evaluate the broader implications of such aid for India's strategic influence and soft power in Southeast Asia.
GS2.imageDescription: An Indian Air Force transport aircraft being loaded with relief supplies, with flood-affected areas visible in the background.
GS2.trivia: The National Disaster Response Force (NDRF) was constituted under the Disaster Management Act, 2005.

GS2.headline: Lok Sabha Passes Bill to Amend Multi-State Cooperative Societies Act
GS2.explainer:
• The Lok Sabha today passed the Multi-State Cooperative Societies (Amendment) Bill, 2024, aimed at improving governance, management, and financial health of cooperative societies.
• The bill introduces provisions for electoral reforms, regularizing elections, and ensuring timely conduct to enhance democratic functioning of cooperatives.
• It also includes measures for financial discipline, supervision, and ease of doing business for multi-state cooperative societies.
• The amendments seek to bring greater transparency and accountability, preventing malpractices and strengthening the cooperative movement in India.
• The bill now awaits consideration and passage in the Rajya Sabha.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
Statutory, regulatory and various quasi-judicial bodies.
prelimsPoints: Lok Sabha passed the Multi-State Cooperative Societies (Amendment) Bill, 2024.
Aims to improve governance, management, and financial health of cooperatives.
Introduces electoral reforms and financial discipline measures.
Bill awaits Rajya Sabha approval.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of the cooperative sector in India's socio-economic development and the need for reforms in the Multi-State Cooperative Societies Act.
Analyze how the proposed amendments aim to address issues of governance, transparency, and financial viability in cooperative societies.
Evaluate the potential impact of these reforms on the democratic functioning and operational efficiency of multi-state cooperatives.
Examine the challenges in implementing such reforms and ensuring their effective adoption by a diverse range of cooperative societies.
GS2.imageDescription: A gavel resting on a parliamentary bill document, symbolizing legislative action.
GS2.trivia: The 97th Constitutional Amendment Act of 2011 gave constitutional status and protection to cooperative societies.

GS2.headline: National Education Policy (NEP) Implementation Review Highlights Digital Learning Progress
GS2.explainer:
• An internal review meeting of the Ministry of Education today highlighted significant progress in the implementation of the digital learning infrastructure under the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020.
• The review noted an increase in the adoption of digital platforms like DIKSHA and SWAYAM, particularly in higher education and teacher training programs.
• Efforts to integrate technology into curriculum development and assessment methods have also shown positive results, enhancing accessibility and quality of education.
• Challenges identified include bridging the digital divide in remote and rural areas, ensuring adequate teacher training, and developing localized digital content.
• The Ministry affirmed its commitment to further strengthening digital education initiatives, including expanding internet connectivity and providing devices to underprivileged students.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
prelimsPoints: NEP 2020 implementation review focuses on digital learning infrastructure.
Progress noted in DIKSHA and SWAYAM platform adoption.
Challenges include digital divide, teacher training, localized content.
Ministry committed to expanding internet connectivity and devices.
mainsPoints: Discuss the transformative potential of digital learning initiatives under NEP 2020 in making education more accessible and equitable in India.
Analyze the successes and challenges faced in integrating technology into the Indian education system, particularly concerning infrastructure and teacher preparedness.
Evaluate the role of platforms like DIKSHA and SWAYAM in promoting continuous learning and skill development across different levels of education.
Suggest policy measures to effectively bridge the digital divide and ensure inclusive access to quality digital education for all students.
GS2.imageDescription: Students in a classroom engaging with digital learning tools on tablets, with a teacher facilitating the session.
GS2.trivia: The National Education Policy 2020 is the first education policy of the 21st century in India.

GS2.headline: G20 Sherpas Meet to Finalize Agenda for Upcoming Summit, India's Priorities Discussed
GS2.explainer:
• G20 Sherpas from member countries convened today in a preparatory meeting to finalize the agenda and draft declarations for the upcoming G20 Leaders' Summit.
• India, as a key member, actively participated, pushing its priorities including sustainable development goals, climate finance, digital public infrastructure, and multilateral reforms.
• Discussions focused on achieving consensus on critical global economic issues, geopolitical challenges, and fostering inclusive growth amidst ongoing international complexities.
• The Sherpas' meeting is crucial for setting the stage for the leaders' deliberations and ensuring productive outcomes from the summit.
• The host nation presented its proposed themes and initiatives, seeking broad support from other G20 members.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate.
prelimsPoints: G20 Sherpas meet to finalize agenda for upcoming Leaders' Summit.
India emphasized priorities like SDGs, climate finance, DPI, multilateral reforms.
Discussions on global economic issues and geopolitical challenges.
Sherpas' meeting is a preparatory step for the main summit.
mainsPoints: Discuss the role and significance of the G20 forum in addressing global economic and geopolitical challenges, particularly from India's perspective.
Analyze India's key priorities at the G20 and how they align with its national interests and global responsibilities.
Evaluate the effectiveness of Sherpa meetings in building consensus and shaping the outcomes of high-level international summits.
Examine the challenges faced by multilateral forums like G20 in achieving unified action amidst diverging national interests and geopolitical tensions.
GS2.imageDescription: A conference room with delegates from various countries seated around a large table, engaging in discussions, with G20 logo visible.
GS2.trivia: The G20 was founded in 1999 in response to the Asian financial crisis, initially as a meeting of finance ministers and central bank governors.

GS3.headline: Government Launches National Mission for Climate-Resilient Agriculture (NMCRA)
GS3.explainer:
• The Union Agriculture Ministry today launched the National Mission for Climate-Resilient Agriculture (NMCRA) to integrate climate adaptation and mitigation strategies into agricultural practices.
• The mission aims to enhance the resilience of Indian agriculture to climate change impacts, focusing on drought-prone, flood-prone, and coastal areas.
• Key components include promoting climate-smart technologies, water-use efficiency, crop diversification, and improved soil health management.
• A significant allocation has been made for research and development into climate-resilient crop varieties and livestock breeds.
• The mission emphasizes community participation and capacity building among farmers, leveraging digital agriculture platforms for better outreach.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment

Major crops - cropping patterns in various parts of the country, - different types of irrigation and irrigation systems storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints; e-technology in the aid of farmers

Issues related to direct and indirect farm subsidies and minimum support prices; PDS— objectives, functioning, limitations, revamping; issues of buffer stocks and food security; Technology missions; economics of animal-rearing.
prelimsPoints: NMCRA is a new mission under the Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare.
Focus areas: Drought, flood, coastal regions.
Components: Climate-smart technologies, water efficiency, crop diversification, soil health.
Aims to enhance resilience and integrate adaptation/mitigation.
mainsPoints: Discuss the necessity of NMCRA in the context of increasing climate variability and its impact on Indian agriculture.
Analyze the potential benefits of NMCRA in enhancing farmers' income and ensuring food security.
Evaluate the challenges in implementing a large-scale mission like NMCRA, including funding, technology adoption, and farmer outreach.
Examine how NMCRA aligns with India's international commitments on climate change and sustainable development goals (SDGs).
GS3.imageDescription: Farmers working in a field with modern, climate-resilient farming techniques, possibly with drip irrigation or protected cultivation.
GS3.trivia: India is the second-largest producer of rice and wheat globally, making climate resilience in agriculture critical for global food supply chains.

GS3.headline: RBI Introduces Stricter Guidelines for Digital Lending Platforms
GS3.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) today released a comprehensive set of new guidelines aimed at regulating digital lending platforms and protecting consumers from predatory practices.
• The guidelines mandate greater transparency in loan disclosures, prohibit automatic increases in credit limits without explicit consent, and introduce a cooling-off period for loan rejections.
• All digital loans must originate from regulated entities (banks/NBFCs), with Lending Service Providers (LSPs) acting only as agents, making them accountable for their actions.
• The new framework addresses concerns regarding exorbitant interest rates, hidden charges, and aggressive recovery tactics often employed by unregulated digital lenders.
• It also strengthens data privacy and security norms, requiring clear consent for data access and prohibiting access to sensitive personal data like contacts or call logs.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Government Budgeting.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
prelimsPoints: RBI's new guidelines target digital lending platforms.
Mandates transparency, explicit consent for credit limit increases.
Loans must originate from Regulated Entities (banks/NBFCs).
Strengthens data privacy and security norms.
mainsPoints: Discuss the necessity of RBI's stricter regulations for digital lending in India, considering the growth of fintech and associated risks.
Analyze how these guidelines balance financial innovation with consumer protection and financial stability.
Examine the potential impact of these regulations on the business model of digital lending platforms and the broader fintech ecosystem.
Suggest further measures that can be taken to ensure responsible digital lending practices in India.
GS3.imageDescription: A smartphone screen displaying a digital lending app with a warning sign about transparent terms and conditions.
GS3.trivia: India's digital lending market is projected to reach over $100 billion by 2025, highlighting the need for robust regulation.

GS3.headline: ISRO Successfully Tests Next-Gen Small Satellite Launch Vehicle (SSLV)
GS3.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) today announced the successful orbital test flight of its new Small Satellite Launch Vehicle (SSLV-D3), marking a significant step towards commercial deployment.
• The SSLV is designed to cater to the burgeoning demand for launching small satellites (up to 500 kg) into Low Earth Orbit (LEO) at a significantly lower cost and quicker turnaround time.
• This third developmental flight successfully deployed three nano-satellites, including an Earth Observation Satellite and two student-built payloads, into their intended orbits.
• The vehicle's modular design and rapid integration capabilities are expected to boost India's share in the global small satellite launch market.
• ISRO aims to make SSLV a 'launch-on-demand' service, appealing to both domestic and international customers for frequent and cost-effective access to space.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
prelimsPoints: ISRO successfully tested SSLV-D3.
SSLV designed for launching small satellites (up to 500 kg) into LEO.
Aims for lower cost and quicker turnaround.
The recent flight deployed three nano-satellites.
Goal is 'launch-on-demand' service.
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic importance of the SSLV program for India's space ambitions and its role in the global commercial space market.
Analyze how SSLV can democratize access to space for smaller entities and contribute to indigenous technological capabilities.
Evaluate the economic potential of ISRO's commercial arm, NewSpace India Limited (NSIL), leveraging technologies like SSLV.
Examine the challenges ISRO faces in competing with private players in the rapidly evolving global space launch market.
GS3.imageDescription: A sleek Small Satellite Launch Vehicle (SSLV) lifting off from the launch pad, leaving a trail of smoke.
GS3.trivia: ISRO's Polar Satellite Launch Vehicle (PSLV) holds a record for launching 104 satellites in a single mission in 2017.

GS3.headline: Report Highlights Alarming Rise in Plastic Pollution in Himalayan Rivers
GS3.explainer:
• A new study published by a consortium of environmental organizations reveals a significant increase in microplastic and macroplastic pollution across major Himalayan river systems.
• The report attributes the surge to unchecked tourism, inadequate waste management infrastructure in mountain towns, and agricultural runoff.
• Scientists warn that plastic pollution poses severe threats to aquatic biodiversity, contaminates water sources for downstream communities, and contributes to soil degradation.
• The study calls for urgent policy interventions, including improved waste collection and recycling facilities, promotion of sustainable tourism, and public awareness campaigns.
• It also emphasizes the transboundary nature of the problem, necessitating regional cooperation among Himalayan nations to address plastic waste effectively.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Disaster and disaster management.
prelimsPoints: Report by environmental organizations on plastic pollution in Himalayan rivers.
Causes: Tourism, inadequate waste management, agricultural runoff.
Impacts: Aquatic biodiversity, water contamination, soil degradation.
Suggests policy interventions and regional cooperation.
mainsPoints: Discuss the multifaceted impacts of plastic pollution on the fragile Himalayan ecosystem and its downstream implications for human health and livelihoods.
Analyze the role of various stakeholders, including tourists, local communities, and government bodies, in contributing to and mitigating plastic waste in the Himalayas.
Suggest comprehensive strategies for sustainable waste management in mountainous regions, incorporating technological solutions and community participation.
Examine the challenges of addressing transboundary environmental issues like river pollution in the Himalayan region.
GS3.imageDescription: A pristine Himalayan riverbank littered with plastic waste, contrasting natural beauty with human impact.
GS3.trivia: The Ganges River, originating in the Himalayas, carries one of the highest plastic loads among global rivers.

GS3.headline: BSF Deploys Advanced Surveillance Technology Along Western Border
GS3.explainer:
• The Border Security Force (BSF) has significantly upgraded its surveillance capabilities along India's western border with Pakistan, deploying a suite of advanced technologies.
• This includes integrated drone systems, long-range thermal imagers, ground surveillance radars, and underground sensors to detect infiltration attempts.
• The move is part of the Comprehensive Integrated Border Management System (CIBMS) aimed at fortifying border security and reducing human patrolling.
• Officials stated that the new systems provide a 24/7, all-weather monitoring capability, enhancing the BSF's response time to threats.
• The deployment comes amidst ongoing concerns about cross-border smuggling of narcotics and weapons, and infiltration by non-state actors.
gsSubTopics: Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism.
Various Security forces and agencies and their mandate.
prelimsPoints: BSF deploys advanced surveillance technology on the western border.
Technologies include drones, thermal imagers, ground radars, underground sensors.
Part of Comprehensive Integrated Border Management System (CIBMS).
Aims for 24/7 all-weather monitoring.
mainsPoints: Discuss the evolving nature of border security challenges in India's western sector and how technological advancements are addressing them.
Analyze the concept and effectiveness of the Comprehensive Integrated Border Management System (CIBMS) in enhancing border security.
Evaluate the role of various surveillance technologies, such as drones and sensors, in preventing infiltration and combating cross-border crime.
Examine the challenges associated with deploying and maintaining advanced technology in challenging border terrains.
GS3.imageDescription: BSF personnel monitoring multiple screens displaying real-time drone footage and radar outputs from the border.
GS3.trivia: The BSF is India's primary border guarding organization on its borders with Pakistan and Bangladesh.

GS3.headline: India's Q1 GDP Growth Shows Robust Performance, Manufacturing Sector Leads
GS3.explainer:
• The National Statistical Office (NSO) today released India's Gross Domestic Product (GDP) figures for the first quarter (April-June) of the financial year 2024-25, showing strong growth.
• The economy expanded by X.X% year-on-year, primarily driven by a robust performance in the manufacturing sector and sustained momentum in services.
• Private consumption expenditure showed signs of recovery, contributing significantly to the overall demand, while government spending remained supportive.
• Analysts noted that despite global headwinds, India's domestic demand and investment cycle continue to be resilient, positioning it as one of the fastest-growing major economies.
• Concerns remain regarding uneven monsoon distribution and its potential impact on agricultural output in subsequent quarters.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Government Budgeting.
prelimsPoints: India's Q1 FY24-25 GDP growth figures released by NSO.
Growth primarily driven by manufacturing and services.
Private consumption showed recovery.
India remains one of the fastest-growing major economies.
mainsPoints: Analyze the key drivers of India's Q1 GDP growth and assess the sustainability of this growth trajectory amidst global economic uncertainties.
Discuss the role of the manufacturing sector in boosting economic growth and its implications for employment generation and 'Make in India' initiatives.
Examine the challenges that could impact India's economic performance in the coming quarters, such as inflation, monsoon variability, and global trade dynamics.
Evaluate the effectiveness of government fiscal and monetary policies in fostering economic stability and growth.
GS3.imageDescription: A graphic displaying India's GDP growth chart with an upward trend, highlighting key economic sectors.
GS3.trivia: India's economy is the fifth-largest in the world by nominal GDP and the third-largest by purchasing power parity (PPP).
